title: Guide to Confession
introduction: The Sacrament of Penance (Confession) is one of the greatest gifts Christ gave to His Church. In this sacrament, your sins are truly forgiven by God through the ministry of the priest. This guide walks you through each step — before, during, and after Confession.

steps.title: Examine Your Conscience
steps.content: Use the Examination of Conscience in this app. Go through each commandment honestly. Note the sins you need to confess, especially any mortal sins. You must confess all mortal sins in kind and number ('I committed this sin X times'). Venial sins should also be confessed but are not strictly required.

steps.title: Make an Act of Contrition
steps.content: Contrition means genuine sorrow for your sins — not just feeling bad about consequences, but sorrow because your sins offend God who is all-good. Perfect contrition (sorrow motivated by love of God) forgives even mortal sins before Confession if you intend to confess them. Imperfect contrition (sorrow motivated by fear of punishment) is sufficient within the sacrament.

steps.title: Resolve to Amend Your Life
steps.content: You must have a firm purpose of amendment — a sincere intention to avoid these sins in the future and to avoid the occasions that lead to them. Without this resolve, the confession is not valid. This does not mean you must be certain you will never sin again — only that you sincerely intend to try.

steps.title: Approach the Confessional
steps.content: You may confess face-to-face or behind a screen — the choice is yours. Kneel (or sit if face-to-face) and wait for the priest to acknowledge you.

steps.title: Begin with the Sign of the Cross
steps.content:
Make the Sign of the Cross and say:

'Bless me, Father, for I have sinned. It has been [length of time] since my last Confession. These are my sins:'

In Latin:
'Benedíc mihi, Pater, quia peccávi. Ab última confessióne mea [tempus] præteríit. Hæc sunt peccáta mea:'

steps.title: Confess Your Sins
steps.content:
Tell the priest your sins simply, honestly, and completely. For mortal sins, state the kind of sin and how many times you committed it. Do not make excuses or blame others. Do not be embarrassed — the priest has heard everything before, and he is bound by the absolute seal of Confession. He cannot reveal your sins to anyone, under any circumstances, ever.

When finished, say: 'For these and all my sins, I am heartily sorry.'

steps.title: Listen to the Priest
steps.content: The priest may offer counsel, ask clarifying questions, or give spiritual advice. Listen attentively. He will then assign a penance — usually prayers to say or a good work to perform.

steps.title: Pray the Act of Contrition
steps.content:
The priest will ask you to pray the Act of Contrition. You may use the traditional form:

'O my God, I am heartily sorry for having offended Thee, and I detest all my sins because I dread the loss of heaven and the pains of hell; but most of all because they offend Thee, my God, Who art all good and deserving of all my love. I firmly resolve, with the help of Thy grace, to confess my sins, to do penance, and to amend my life. Amen.'

steps.title: Receive Absolution
steps.content:
The priest will pray the words of absolution. In the Traditional Rite:

'Dominus noster Iesus Christus te absolvat; et ego auctoritate ipsius te absolvo ab omni vinculo excommunicationis et interdicti in quantum possum et tu indiges. Deinde, ego te absolvo a peccatis tuis in nomine Patris, et Filii, ✠ et Spiritus Sancti. Amen.'

At this moment, your sins are truly, completely, and eternally forgiven. You are restored to the state of grace.

steps.title: Perform Your Penance
steps.content: Complete the penance the priest assigned as soon as possible — preferably immediately, while still in the church. The penance is part of the sacrament and helps make reparation for the damage your sins have caused.

steps.title: Give Thanks
steps.content: Spend a few minutes in prayer thanking God for the grace of forgiveness. You have just received one of the most powerful sacraments in the Church. Your soul is clean. If you have been away from Confession for a long time, this is a moment of profound joy.

steps.title: Avoid the Occasions of Sin
steps.content: Identify the people, places, situations, and habits that lead you to sin, and take concrete steps to avoid them. This is the 'firm purpose of amendment' in action. If you keep falling into the same sins, discuss strategies with your confessor — that is part of his ministry.

frequentConfession.title: How Often Should I Confess?
frequentConfession.content: The Church requires Confession at least once a year (and whenever in mortal sin before receiving Communion). However, the saints unanimously recommend frequent Confession — weekly or bi-weekly. Pope Pius XII called frequent Confession 'a practice that was introduced into the Church by the inspiration of the Holy Ghost.' Even if you have only venial sins, regular Confession strengthens your soul against temptation and deepens your relationship with God.
